875	\section{The \(\mathrm{GL}_d\) bundle transition cocycle}
876	\label{sec:grquot_bundle_cocycle}
877	
878	The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}) is
879	obtained by feeding the gluing primitive \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} the per-chart free
880	rank-\(d\) sheaves \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\), the bundle transitions
881	\(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\), and the two module cocycle hypotheses (C1)/(C2) that primitive
882	requires. The matrix cocycle for the Cramer inverses \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) is already in hand --
883	it is the content of the scheme-level cocycle \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} and the minor identities
884	\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities} -- but the gluing primitive consumes its
885	\emph{module-level} incarnation: an honest isomorphism of sheaves of modules together with
886	its self-identity and triple-overlap multiplicativity. The three blocks of this section
887	package exactly that, decoupling the construction of \(\mathcal{U}\) from the remaining
888	matrix-to-module transport bookkeeping. This is the only net-new infrastructure the
889	universal quotient requires beyond the gluing primitive itself.

902	  \uses{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}

904	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}), and \(1^{-1} = 1\).

915	  the overlap immersion and transition of the chart cover (\cref{def:gr_transition}). The
916	  \emph{bundle transition} \(g_{I,J}\) is the isomorphism of sheaves of modules on the
917	  overlap \(U^I_J\)

938	  the matrix automorphism into the displayed isomorphism. Invertibility of the matrix makes

1040	\begin{lemma}[Tensor-power comparison isomorphism]
1041	  \label{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
1042	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPowAdd}
1043	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
1044	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules"
1045	  % (Tag 01CU, following `definition-powers') (read from
1046	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4249-L4254).
1047	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1048	  % "With this definition we have canonical isomorphisms
1049	  %  $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
1050	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \to
1051	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m}$, and these isomorphisms
1052	  %  satisfy a commutativity and an associativity constraint
1053	  %  (formulation omitted)."
1054	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (canonical
1055	  power isomorphisms).}
1056	  For a line bundle \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(m, m' \in \mathbb{N}\) there is a
1057	  canonical isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1058	  \[
1059	    \mu_{m,m'} :
1060	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1061	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m')},
1062	  \]
1063	  natural in \(\mathcal{L}\), and these isomorphisms satisfy a commutativity
1064	  constraint (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) agrees with \(\mu_{m',m}\) after the braiding) and
1065	  an associativity constraint (the two ways of combining
1066	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
1067	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''}\) into \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\) agree).
1068	\end{lemma}
1069	
1070	\begin{proof}
1071	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj, cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc,
1072	    def:tensorObjUnitIso, def:tensorBraiding,
1073	    lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
1074	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1075	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1076	  We do \emph{not} build a full monoidal structure on the whole category
1077	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) --- none is constructed here --- but only assemble
1078	  the comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\) from those structural isomorphisms of
1079	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} that the induction actually uses: the associator,
1080	  the braiding, and the left unitor.
1081	
1082	  \textbf{The structural maps.} The braiding and the unitors descend to
1083	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) directly: each is the sheafification of the
1084	  corresponding isomorphism of the presheaf symmetric monoidal structure
1085	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and sheafification, being a
1086	  functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. The \emph{associator} is not
1087	  the sheafification of a single presheaf isomorphism, because the iterated
1088	  sheaf tensor products
1089	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}\) and
1090	  \(\mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})\) each carry an inner
1091	  sheafification (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); it is supplied instead by
1092	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, whose construction clears those inner
1093	  sheafifications using the strong-monoidality comparison
1094	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}. These are the only
1095	  isomorphisms of sheaves of modules the construction invokes; right-exactness
1096	  of the tensor product is never used.
1097	
1098	  \textbf{Construction of \(\mu_{m,m'}\).} Fix \(m'\) and induct on \(m\). For
1099	  \(m = 0\),
1100	  \[
1101	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1102	      = \mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1103	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1104	  \]
1105	  is the left unitor of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}. For the inductive step the
1106	  recursive definition
1107	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}\)
1108	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}) gives
1109	  \[
1110	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1111	    = (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}) \otimes
1112	      \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1113	    \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
1114	      (\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'})
1115	    \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{id} \otimes \beta\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
1116	      (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes \mathcal{L}),
1117	  \]
1118	  using the associator \(\alpha\) (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and then the
1119	  braiding \(\beta\) of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} whiskered into the left
1120	  factor. The inverse associator \(\alpha^{-1}\)
1121	  (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and the inductive comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\)
1122	  whiskered by \(\mathcal{L}\) on the right then carry this to
1123	  \[
1124	    (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}) \otimes
1125	      \mathcal{L}
1126	    \xrightarrow{\ \mu_{m,m'} \rhd \mathcal{L}\ }
1127	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m')} \otimes \mathcal{L}
1128	    = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m + m') + 1)},
1129	  \]
1130	  the final equality being the recursive definition. The reindexing
1131	  \((m + m') + 1 = (m + 1) + m'\) identifies the target with
1132	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m+1) + m')}\), and the composite is
1133	  \(\mu_{m+1,m'}\), an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
1134	  \(\mathcal{L}\) holds because each constituent comparison is natural.
1135	
1136	  \textbf{Commutativity and associativity constraints.} The braiding and
1137	  unitors are the sheafifications of the symmetric-monoidal coherence data of
1138	  \(\PshMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and
1139	  the associator is intertwined with the presheaf associator by the comparison
1140	  isomorphisms of \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} through the
1141	  construction of \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}. Both the commutativity square
1142	  (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) versus \(\mu_{m',m}\) postcomposed with the braiding) and the
1143	  associativity pentagon (the two bracketings of
1144	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
1145	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''} \to \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\)) are therefore
1146	  images, under the sheafification functor together with these comparison
1147	  isomorphisms, of the corresponding coherence diagrams of the presheaf
1148	  symmetric monoidal structure, which commute by Mac\,Lane coherence. Since a
1149	  functor preserves commuting diagrams and the comparison isomorphisms are
1150	  natural, the constraints hold for the family \(\{\mu_{m,m'}\}\). We emphasise
1151	  that this argument uses only the single strong-monoidality comparison
1152	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} and the resulting associator
1153	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, not a full monoidal structure on
1154	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
1155	\end{proof}

1157	\section{Lax-monoidal global sections}
1158	\label{sec:sgr_lax_sections}
1159	
1160	The graded multiplication is supplied by the lax-monoidal structure of the
1161	global-sections functor \(\Gamma(X, -) : \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
1162	\mathrm{ModuleCat}(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X))\). It is only \emph{lax} because
1163	global sections do not commute with sheafification: a global section of a
1164	sheafified tensor product is not in general a finite sum of elementary tensors of
1165	global sections.
1166	
1167	\begin{definition}\leanok
1168	[Section multiplication]
1169	  \label{def:sectionMul}
1170	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul}
1171	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1172	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1173	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
1174	  \emph{section multiplication} is the \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-bilinear map
1175	  \[
1176	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)}
1177	      \Gamma(X, \mathcal{G})
1178	    \;\longrightarrow\;
1179	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})
1180	  \]
1181	  defined as follows. A pair of global sections \((\sigma, \tau)\) determines,
1182	  by the objectwise formula of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} at the
1183	  top open, the elementary tensor \(\sigma \otimes \tau \in
1184	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})(X)\), a global section of
1185	  the tensor product presheaf. Composing with the global-sections component of
1186	  the sheafification unit
1187	  \(\eta : \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
1188	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^{\#} =
1189	  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\)
1190	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
1191	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}) yields a global section
1192	  \(\sigma \cdot \tau \in
1193	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\). Bilinearity over
1194	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is inherited from bilinearity of the elementary
1195	  tensor and \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-linearity of \(\eta\).
1196	\end{definition}
1197	
1198	\begin{lemma}[Coherence of the section multiplication]
1199	  \label{lem:sectionMul_coherent}
1200	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_assoc_unit}
1201	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib, lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
1202	  The section multiplications (\cref{def:sectionMul}) are associative and unital
1203	  in the following sense. For sheaves \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{H}\)
1204	  the two composites
1205	  \[
1206	    \Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{G}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{H})
1207	    \longrightarrow
1208	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H}\bigr)
1209	    \cong
1210	    \Gamma\bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes (\mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H})\bigr)
1211	  \]
1212	  agree after transport along the associator of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}; and
1213	  the unit element \(1 \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
1214	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) acts as a two-sided identity, i.e.\ the section
1215	  multiplication by \(1\) is, after the unitor isomorphism
1216	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{F} \cong \mathcal{F}\), the identity on
1217	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\).
1218	\end{lemma}
1219	
1220	\begin{proof}
1221	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1222	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1223	  Both statements reduce to the corresponding identities at the level of the
1224	  tensor product presheaf, where by \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
1225	  evaluation at the top open is strictly monoidal, so the associator and unitors
1226	  act on elementary tensors by the usual formulas
1227	  \((\sigma \otimes \tau) \otimes \upsilon \mapsto
1228	  \sigma \otimes (\tau \otimes \upsilon)\) and
1229	  \(1 \otimes \sigma \mapsto \sigma\). The sheafification unit \(\eta\) is a
1230	  natural transformation, so it commutes with the associator and unitor
1231	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); applying \(\Gamma(X,-)\)
1232	  to the naturality squares transports these presheaf-level identities to the
1233	  section multiplications.
1234	\end{proof}

1236	\section{Graded-ring and graded-module assembly}
1237	\label{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}
1238	
1239	The components and multiplications of the previous two sections are assembled
1240	into the graded ring and graded module through Mathlib's
1241	\emph{external-direct-sum} graded API: the relevant inputs are families of
1242	abelian groups indexed by \(\mathbb{N}\) together with multiplication maps
1243	landing in the index-sum component, not submodules of a pre-existing ring.
1244	
1245	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded commutative semiring]
1246	  \label{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
1247	  \lean{DirectSum.GCommSemiring}
1248	  \mathlibok
1249	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.DirectSum.Ring}).}
1250	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
1251	  monoids equipped with a graded multiplication
1252	  \(A_i \times A_j \to A_{i+j}\) and a unit \(1 \in A_0\) satisfying graded
1253	  associativity, two-sided unitality, distributivity, and graded commutativity
1254	  (the data of a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\) on \(A\)). Then the external direct
1255	  sum \(\bigoplus_i A_i\) is a commutative semiring, with multiplication the
1256	  bilinear extension of the graded multiplication and identity the image of
1257	  \(1 \in A_0\).
1258	\end{lemma}
1259	
1260	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded module]
1261	  \label{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}
1262	  \lean{DirectSum.Gmodule}
1263	  \mathlibok
1264	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Module.GradedModule}).}
1265	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) carry a graded semiring structure as
1266	  in \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib} and let
1267	  \(M : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
1268	  monoids with a graded scalar action \(A_i \times M_j \to M_{i+j}\) satisfying
1269	  the graded module axioms (the data of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\) of \(M\) over
1270	  \(A\)). Then the external direct sum \(\bigoplus_j M_j\) is a module over the
1271	  semiring \(\bigoplus_i A_i\), with scalar multiplication the bilinear
1272	  extension of the graded action.
1273	\end{lemma}

1275	\begin{lemma}[Graded semiring structure on the section components]
1276	  \label{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
1277	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
1278	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
1279	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib,
1280	    lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
1281	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
1282	  % preceding `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
1283	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4257-L4267).
1284	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1285	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. We can define a $\mathbf{Z}$-graded
1286	  %  ring structure on $\bigoplus \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ by mapping
1287	  %  $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ and
1288	  %  $t \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m})$ to the
1289	  %  section corresponding to $s \otimes t$ in
1290	  %  $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m})$.
1291	  %  We omit the verification that this defines a commutative
1292	  %  and associative ring with $1$."
1293	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
1294	  ring structure on \(\bigoplus \Gamma(X,\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})\)).}
1295	  Let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on \(X_s\). The family of
1296	  \(\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})\)-modules
1297	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) carries a graded commutative
1298	  semiring structure (a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\),
1299	  \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}) whose degree-\((m,m')\)
1300	  multiplication is the composite
1301	  \[
1302	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
1303	      \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'})
1304	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
1305	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'}\bigr)
1306	    \xrightarrow{\ \Gamma(\mu_{m,m'})\ }
1307	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
1308	  \]
1309	  the section multiplication (\cref{def:sectionMul}) followed by the
1310	  tensor-power comparison isomorphism (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), with unit
1311	  \(1 \in \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s}) = \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes 0})\)
1312	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}). Consequently
1313	  \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
1314	  commutative semiring; this is the graded-ring structure underlying
1315	  \cref{def:sectionGradedRing}.
1316	\end{lemma}
1317	
1318	\begin{proof}
1319	  \uses{lem:sheafTensorPow_add, lem:sectionMul_coherent,
1320	    lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
1321	  The graded multiplication maps are the composites displayed in the statement.
1322	  Graded associativity is the associativity coherence of
1323	  \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} combined with the associativity constraint of
1324	  the comparison isomorphisms \(\mu\) (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}); graded
1325	  commutativity is the unit/commutativity of the section multiplication combined
1326	  with the commutativity constraint of \(\mu\); two-sided unitality is the unit
1327	  coherence of \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} together with
1328	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\). These are exactly the axioms of a
1329	  \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\), so \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
1330	  endows \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) with its
1331	  commutative-semiring structure.
1332	\end{proof}

1334	\begin{lemma}[Graded module structure on the twisted-section components]
1335	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_gmodule}
1336	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_gmodule}
1337	  \uses{def:sheafModuleTwist, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
1338	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib,
1339	    lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
1340	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
1341	  % `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
1342	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4279-L4286).
1343	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1344	  % "Given a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{F}$ we set
1345	  %  $$
1346	  %  \Gamma_*(X, \mathcal{L}, \mathcal{F})
1347	  %  =
1348	  %  \bigoplus\nolimits_{n \in \mathbf{Z}} \Gamma(X,
1349	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})
1350	  %  $$
1351	  %  which we think of as a graded $\Gamma_*(X, \mathcal{L})$-module."
1352	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
1353	  module \(\Gamma_*(X,\mathcal{L},\mathcal{F})\)).}
1354	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf and \(L_s\) a line bundle on
1355	  \(X_s\). The family
1356	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
1357	  (\cref{def:sheafModuleTwist}) carries a graded module structure (a
1358	  \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\), \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}) over the graded
1359	  semiring of \cref{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}, with degree-\((m,m')\)
1360	  action
1361	  \[
1362	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
1363	      \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
1364	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
1365	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes
1366	      (\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})\bigr)
1367	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1368	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
1369	  \]
1370	  the section multiplication followed by the comparison isomorphism that
1371	  reassociates and merges the line-bundle factors (using
1372	  \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add} and the symmetry of the tensor product).
1373	  Consequently \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
1374	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a graded module over
1375	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\); this is the graded-module structure underlying
1376	  \cref{def:sectionGradedModule}.
1377	\end{lemma}
1378	
1379	\begin{proof}
1380	  \uses{lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:sheafTensorPow_add,
1381	    lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib, lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
1382	  The action maps are the displayed composites. The graded-module axioms ---
1383	  compatibility of the action with the ring multiplication
1384	  (\(r \cdot (r' \cdot x) = (r r') \cdot x\)) and unitality
1385	  (\(1 \cdot x = x\)) --- follow from the associativity and unit coherences of
1386	  the section multiplication (\cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent}) together with the
1387	  associativity and commutativity constraints of the comparison isomorphisms
1388	  (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), exactly as in the proof of
1389	  \cref{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring} but with the third tensor factor
1390	  carrying \(\mathcal{F}_s\). These are the axioms of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\), so
1391	  \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib} produces the module structure of
1392	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over \(R(X_s, L_s)\).
1393	\end{proof}

980	\begin{lemma}\leanok
981	[Cramer-inverse cocycle on the triple overlap]
982	  \label{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix}
983	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.bundleTransition_cocycle_matrix}
984	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq,
985	    lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col,
986	    lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel, lem:gr_cocycle}
987	  Over the common triple-overlap ring (the localisation carrying the three charts'
988	  data to \(V_{IJK}\)) the Cramer inverses of the localised minors satisfy the
989	  multiplicative cocycle identity
990	  \[
991	    (X^J_K)^{-1}\,(X^I_J)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1}.
992	  \]
993	  This is the pure matrix-algebra core of (C2), independent of any sheaf data.
994	\end{lemma}
995	\begin{proof}
996	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq,
997	    lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col,
998	    lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel, lem:gr_cocycle}
999	  Write all matrices over the common triple-overlap ring. The image matrix of the
1000	  transition \(\theta_{I,J}\) is \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\), and its \(K\)-minor is
1001	  computed by restricting to the \(K\)-indexed columns; since taking a column
1002	  submatrix commutes with left multiplication (\cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}),
1003	  \[
1004	    X^J_K \;=\; \bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr)_K \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_K .
1005	  \]
1006	  Both \(X^I_J\) and \(X^I_K\) have unit determinant
1007	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}), so this product of invertibles inverts
1008	  by \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}\):
1009	  \[
1010	    (X^J_K)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1}\,(X^I_J).
1011	  \]
1012	  Multiplying on the right by \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) and cancelling \(X^I_J (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1\)
1013	  via the two-sided Cramer identity (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}, the
1014	  cancellation packaged in \cref{lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}) gives
1015	  \((X^J_K)^{-1} (X^I_J)^{-1} = (X^I_K)^{-1}\). The agreement of the three image
1016	  matrices over the triple overlap that licenses these manipulations is the content of
1017	  \cref{lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}, the matrix form of the scheme-level cocycle
1018	  \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}.
1019	\end{proof}

1021	\begin{lemma}[Scalar endomorphism is natural under pullback]
1022	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_pullback}
1023	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_pullback}
1024	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:modules_pullbackComp}
1025	  Let \(p : T \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and \(a \in \Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S)\) a
1026	  global function, with comorphism on global sections
1027	  \(p^{\sharp} : \Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S) \to \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)\).
1028	  Write \(q : p^{*}\mathbf{1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathbf{1}\) for the unit-pullback comparison
1029	  (\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} at a one-element index). Then the pullback of the scalar endomorphism
1030	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)\) is conjugate, through \(q\), to the scalar endomorphism of the
1031	  base-changed function:
1032	  \[
1033	    p^{*}\bigl(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)\bigr)
1034	      \;=\; q^{-1} \,\circ\, \mathrm{scalarEnd}\bigl(p^{\sharp} a\bigr) \,\circ\, q
1035	  \]
1036	  as endomorphisms of \(p^{*}\mathbf{1}\); equivalently
1037	  \(q \circ p^{*}(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)) = \mathrm{scalarEnd}(p^{\sharp} a) \circ q\). This is the
1038	  single-entry naturality atom underlying the matrix-level statement
1039	  \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback}.
1040	\end{lemma}
1041	\begin{proof}
1042	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1043	  The scalar endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)\) (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}) is multiplication
1044	  by the global section \(a\) on the unit module \(\mathbf 1 = \mathcal{O}_S\). Pulling back
1045	  along \(p\) sends multiplication by \(a\) to multiplication by its image, and the comorphism
1046	  \(p^{\sharp}\) is precisely the action of pullback on global functions, so
1047	  \(p^{*}(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a))\) is multiplication by \(p^{\sharp}a\) on \(p^{*}\mathbf 1\). The
1048	  comparison \(q\) is an isomorphism of unit modules intertwining this multiplication on
1049	  \(p^{*}\mathbf 1\) with multiplication by \(p^{\sharp}a\) on \(\mathbf 1\); conjugating back
1050	  along \(q\) therefore yields the claimed identity. The verification is a single computation on
1051	  the unit module, where multiplication by a function commutes with the natural comparison
1052	  \(q\).
1053	\end{proof}

1055	\begin{lemma}[Matrix endomorphism is natural under pullback]
1056	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback}
1057	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEnd_pullback}
1058	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, def:gr_scalarEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_pullback, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
1059	    def:modules_pullbackComp}
1060	  Let \(p : T \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), and
1061	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\). Let
1062	  \(p^{\sharp}M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(\Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T))\) be the entrywise
1063	  image of \(M\) under the comorphism \(p^{\sharp}\), and let
1064	  \(Q : p^{*}(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}\) be the free-pullback
1065	  comparison \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} at the rank-\(d\) index set. Then the
1066	  pullback of the matrix endomorphism \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) is conjugate, through \(Q\), to
1067	  the matrix endomorphism of the base-changed matrix:
1068	  \[
1069	    p^{*}\bigl(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\bigr)
1070	      \;=\; Q^{-1} \,\circ\, \mathrm{matrixEnd}(p^{\sharp}M) \,\circ\, Q .
1071	  \]
1072	\end{lemma}
1073	\begin{proof}
1074	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_pullback, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
1075	    def:modules_pullbackComp}
1076	  By \cref{def:gr_matrixEnd}, \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) is the biproduct-assembled endomorphism
1077	  of \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d} = \bigoplus_{k} \mathbf 1\) whose \((k,\ell)\)-component is
1078	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(M_{k,\ell})\). The pullback functor \(p^{*}\) is a left adjoint, hence
1079	  additive and biproduct-preserving; through the coproduct comparison underlying the
1080	  free-pullback isomorphism \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} it carries the biproduct presentation
1081	  of \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) to that of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}\) and the assembled morphism to the
1082	  morphism reassembled from the pulled-back components, the reassociation of the iterated
1083	  pullbacks being \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. On each single \((k,\ell)\)-component the
1084	  pulled-back scalar endomorphism is, by \cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_pullback}, the
1085	  comparison-conjugate of \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(p^{\sharp}M_{k,\ell}) =
1086	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}((p^{\sharp}M)_{k,\ell})\). Reassembling over the biproduct, the conjugating
1087	  one-element comparisons combine into the single free comparison \(Q\), giving
1088	  \(p^{*}(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)) = Q^{-1}\circ\mathrm{matrixEnd}(p^{\sharp}M)\circ Q\).
1089	\end{proof}

1091	\begin{lemma}[Base-change bridge to the localised cocycle ring homomorphisms]
1092	  \label{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge}
1093	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.baseChange_bridge}
1094	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_the_glue_data,
1095	    lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_incl_left,
1096	    def:gr_away_incl_right}
1097	  Fix indices \(I, J, K\) and the triple overlap \(V_{IJK}\) of the chart cover
1098	  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}, \cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}).
1099	  The scheme-pullback base-change map on global sections
1100	  \(\Gamma(U^I_J, \mathcal{O}) \to \Gamma(V_{IJK}, \mathcal{O})\) induced by the first projection
1101	  \(p^{IJ}_{IJK}\), and likewise the maps induced by the second projection and by the triple
1102	  transition \(t'_{IJK}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}), are identified -- through the natural identification of global sections of an affine scheme
1103	  with its coordinate ring and the fact that the projections and triple transition are the
1104	  \(\Spec\) morphisms of the coordinate-ring homomorphisms by which the Grassmannian glue datum
1105	  (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) is assembled -- with the ring homomorphisms
1106	  \(\mathrm{awayInclLeft}\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_left}),
1107	  \(\mathrm{awayInclRight}\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_right}) and \(\Theta_{IJ}\)
1108	  (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}) over which the matrix cocycle
1109	  \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix} is stated. Consequently the entrywise image of a Cramer
1110	  inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) under the geometric base-change map agrees with its image under the
1111	  corresponding coordinate-ring homomorphism, so \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix} applies
1112	  to the base-changed matrices.
1113	\end{lemma}
1114	\begin{proof}
1115	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_the_glue_data, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij,
1116	    def:gr_away_incl_left, def:gr_away_incl_right}
1117	  The charts \(U^I\) and their overlaps are affine, identified with the spectra of the localised
1118	  coordinate rings; under this identification the global-sections comorphism of a morphism of
1119	  affine charts is the ring homomorphism that induces it on spectra. The triple overlap
1120	  \(V_{IJK}\) is the spectrum of the triple-overlap localisation, and the two projections and
1121	  the triple transition \(t'_{IJK}\) of the glue datum (\cref{def:gr_transition},
1122	  \cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) are, on charts, the spectra of \(\mathrm{awayInclLeft}\),
1123	  \(\mathrm{awayInclRight}\) and \(\Theta_{IJ}\) respectively -- the precise form in which the
1124	  chart transitions and glue datum are assembled from the transition maps. Applying the
1125	  global-sections identification to each morphism translates its geometric base-change map on
1126	  global sections into the named ring homomorphism, whence the entrywise images of the Cramer
1127	  inverses coincide.
1128	\end{proof}

1130	\begin{lemma}[Transport and endpoint alignment of the bundle transitions]
1131	  \label{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_transport}
1132	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.bundleTransition_cocycle_transport}
1133	  \uses{def:gr_bundleTransition, lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix, lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul,

1135	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_glueData_bridges,
1136	    def:modules_pullbackComp, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1137	  Fix indices \(I, J, K\) with triple overlap \(V_{IJK} = U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K\),
1138	  and let \(\widehat{g}_{IJ}^{\,K}, \widehat{g}_{JK}^{\,I}, \widehat{g}_{IK}^{\,J}\) be
1139	  the base-change transports (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) of the
1140	  three bundle transitions to \(V_{IJK}\). Under the free-pullback comparisons
1141	  (\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}) and the endpoint bridges
1142	  (\cref{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}), each transported transition is identified with the
1143	  matrix automorphism of the corresponding base-changed Cramer inverse on the common
1144	  free sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{V_{IJK}}^{\,d}\); granting the matrix identity
1145	  \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix}, the composite satisfies
1146	  \[
1147	    \widehat{g}_{JK}^{\,I} \circ \widehat{g}_{IJ}^{\,K}
1148	      \;=\; \widehat{g}_{IK}^{\,J}
1149	  \]
1150	  as isomorphisms of sheaves of modules on \(V_{IJK}\). This is the substantive
1151	  transport step: the alignment of the three transports' domains and codomains.
1152	\end{lemma}
1153	\begin{proof}
1154	  \uses{def:gr_bundleTransition, lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix, lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul,
1155	    lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback, lem:gr_baseChange_bridge,
1156	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_glueData_bridges,
1157	    def:modules_pullbackComp, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1158	  This is pure assembly of the two infrastructure pieces over the endpoint bridges. Each
1159	  bundle transition is, by \cref{def:gr_bundleTransition}, \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}\) of a
1160	  localised Cramer inverse conjugated by the free-pullback comparisons
1161	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}; its base-change transport
1162	  (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) reassociates the iterated pullbacks via
1163	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}, and the three endpoint bridges \cref{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
1164	  pin the source, middle and target sheaves to a single \(\mathcal{O}_{V_{IJK}}^{\,d}\). By
1165	  \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback} each transport then equals \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}\) of the
1166	  base-changed Cramer inverse, and by \cref{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge} those base-changed matrices
1167	  are exactly \((X^I_J)^{-1}, (X^J_K)^{-1}, (X^I_K)^{-1}\) over the common triple-overlap ring of
1168	  \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix}. The linkage \cref{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul} composes the
1169	  first two to \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}\bigl((X^J_K)^{-1}(X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr)\), and
1170	  by the matrix cocycle identity \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix} the argument equals
1171	  \((X^I_K)^{-1}\), so the composite equals \(\widehat{g}_{IK}^{\,J}\); the bridges make both
1172	  sides isomorphisms between the same pair of sheaves on \(V_{IJK}\).
1173	\end{proof}

1175	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1176	[Triple-overlap multiplicativity of the bundle transition (C2)]
1177	  \label{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_mul}
1178	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.bundleTransition_cocycle}
1179	  \uses{def:gr_bundleTransition, lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix, lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul,
1180	    lem:gr_bundleCocycle_transport, lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport,
1181	    lem:gr_glueData_bridges, def:modules_pullbackComp}
1182	  Over each triple overlap \(V_{IJK} = U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K\) the base-change transports
1183	  (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) of the three bundle transitions satisfy
1184	  \[
1185	    \widehat{g}_{JK}^{\,I} \circ \widehat{g}_{IJ}^{\,K}
1186	      \;=\; \widehat{g}_{IK}^{\,J},
1187	  \]
1188	  the module-level form of the descent condition (C2) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} for
1189	  the bundle data \((g_{I,J})\). This is the \(\mathrm{GL}_d\)-valued multiplicative cocycle
1190	  of \(\mathcal{U}\).

1193	  \uses{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix, lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul,
1194	    lem:gr_bundleCocycle_transport}

1196	  \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix} establishes the Cramer-inverse cocycle
1197	  \((X^J_K)^{-1}(X^I_J)^{-1} = (X^I_K)^{-1}\) over the triple overlap. Second, the linkage
1198	  \cref{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul} records that composition of matrix automorphisms realises
1199	  matrix multiplication. Third, the transport step \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_transport}
1200	  carries the three transitions to the common overlap \(V_{IJK}\), aligns their source,
1201	  middle, and target sheaves through the base-change transport and the endpoint bridges, and
1202	  -- feeding it the matrix identity through the linkage -- yields exactly the stated equality
1203	  \(\widehat{g}_{JK}^{\,I} \circ \widehat{g}_{IJ}^{\,K} = \widehat{g}_{IK}^{\,J}\).

1217	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1218	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
1219	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L903-L910).
1220	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1221	  % "Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
1222	  %  for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
1223	  %  $(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$

1225	  %  $$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$"

1227	  Over each overlap \(U^I_J = \Spec R^I_J\) define the bundle-transition matrix
1228	  \[
1229	    g_{I,J} \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} \;\in\; \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J),
1230	  \]
1231	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
1232	  which is invertible because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\). The matrices
1233	  \((g_{I,J})\) are compatible with the chart-gluing cocycle \((\theta_{I,J})\)
1234	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}): over a triple overlap they
1235	  satisfy \(g_{I,K} = g_{J,K}\, g_{I,J}\) and \(g_{I,I} = 1\), the multiplicative
1236	  cocycle condition for a vector bundle. Gluing the free rank-\(d\) sheaves

1238	  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) therefore

1240	  \(\mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). Its determinant line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\)

1246	
1247	\leanok
1248	[The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}^{r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\)]
1249	  \label{def:tautological_quotient}
1250	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient}
1251	  \uses{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_chart_quotient,
1252	    def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle,
1253	    def:scheme_modules_glue, def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso,
1254	    def:gr_modules_glueHom}
1255	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1256	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
1257	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L908-L910).
1258	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1259	  % "This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$,
1260	  %  so we get a surjective homomorphism
1261	  %  $u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$."
1262	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1263	  The chart quotients \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
1264	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) are compatible with the bundle gluing
1265	  \((g_{I,J})\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}): over
1266	  each overlap \(U^I_J\) one has the identity of \(d \times r\) matrices
1267	  \[
1268	    g_{I,J}\, X^I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I \;=\; X^J \qquad \text{on } U^I_J,
1269	  \]
1270	  which is precisely the defining formula of the chart transition
1271	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}).
1272	  Hence the local maps \(u^I\), transported through the trivialisations of
1273	  \(\mathcal{U}\), agree on overlaps and glue to a single surjective
1274	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-linear homomorphism
1275	  \[
1276	    u \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
1277	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathcal{U},
1278	  \]
1279	  the \emph{tautological} (or \emph{universal}) rank-\(d\) quotient of the trivial
1280	  rank-\(r\) sheaf. It is surjective because each \(u^I\) is (split) surjective and
1281	  surjectivity is local on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally
1282	  free of rank \(d\) by \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}. The pair
1283	  \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is the family of rank-\(d\) quotients carried
1284	  by \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself.

1287	\section{The functor of points}
1288	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
1289	
1290	The Grassmannian functor sends a scheme \(T\) to the set of \emph{equivalence classes} of
1291	rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). The following block packages a single such
1292	quotient, the equivalence relation on them, and the pullback action through which the
1293	functor acts on morphisms; the two coherence blocks after it isolate the comparison
1294	identities that make that action functorial.
1295	
1296	\begin{definition}\leanok
1297	[Rank-\(d\) quotients and their pullback action]
1298	  \label{def:gr_rankQuotient}
1299	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient,
1300	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.Rel,
1301	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_refl,
1302	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_symm,
1303	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_trans,
1304	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqSetoid,
1305	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback,
1306	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback_rel}
1307	  \uses{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
1308	        def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
1309	  A \emph{rank-\(d\) quotient of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} on a scheme \(T\) is a triple
1310	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-modules
1311	  \(\mathcal{F}\) that is locally free of rank \(d\) (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank})
1312	  together with an epimorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) out
1313	  of the trivial rank-\(r\) bundle. Two such quotients \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\)
1314	  and \(y = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) are \emph{equivalent}, written \(x \sim y\),
1315	  when there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with
1316	  \(q \circ f = q'\) (equivalently \(\ker q = \ker q'\)). This relation is reflexive
1317	  (\(f = \mathrm{id}\)), symmetric (\(f \mapsto f^{-1}\)), and transitive (composition of the
1318	  witnessing isomorphisms), hence an equivalence relation -- the setoid whose quotient is the
1319	  functor's value at \(T\).
1320	
1321	  For a morphism \(\psi : T' \to T\) the \emph{pullback action} sends
1322	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) to
1323	  \[
1324	    \psi^{*}x \;=\;
1325	      \bigl\langle\, \psi^{*}\mathcal{F},\;
1326	        \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,r} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1327	        \psi^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ \psi^{*}q\ }
1328	        \psi^{*}\mathcal{F} \,\bigr\rangle,
1329	  \]
1330	  re-presenting the source as the trivial bundle via the free-pullback isomorphism
1331	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}. The result is again a rank-\(d\) quotient: the composite is
1332	  an epimorphism because pullback, a left adjoint, preserves epimorphisms and the
1333	  re-presentation is an isomorphism, and \(\psi^{*}\mathcal{F}\) is locally free of rank \(d\)
1334	  by \cref{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}. The pullback action respects the equivalence
1335	  relation -- given \(f\) witnessing \(x \sim y\), the pulled-back isomorphism \(\psi^{*}f\)
1336	  witnesses \(\psi^{*}x \sim \psi^{*}y\), by functoriality of pullback -- so it descends to a
1337	  map of equivalence classes.
1338	\end{definition}
1339	
1340	The two blocks below record the comparison identities, between the free-pullback isomorphism
1341	\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} and Mathlib's pseudofunctor structure on pullback, that make
1342	the descended pullback action of \cref{def:gr_rankQuotient} functorial.
1343	
1344	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1345	[Identity coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
1346	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1347	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1348	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1349	  For the identity morphism \(\mathrm{id}_T\), the canonical comparison morphism of unit
1350	  modules \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) -- the map underlying the
1351	  free-pullback isomorphism \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} at a one-element index -- coincides
1352	  with the component at \(\mathbf{1}\) of the pullback-identity isomorphism
1353	  \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*} \cong \mathrm{id}\).
1354	\end{lemma}
1355	
1356	\begin{proof}
1357	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1358	  The comparison morphism is the mate of \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf 1}\) under the
1359	  pullback--pushforward adjunction: the image of the identity of \(\mathbf 1\) through the
1360	  adjunction's hom-equivalence on the unit. For the identity scheme morphism the pushforward
1361	  is the identity functor, so the adjunction unit is itself an identity; Mathlib's
1362	  pullback-identity isomorphism is exactly the conjugate (left-adjoint transpose) of the
1363	  pushforward-identity isomorphism. Tracing the identity through the hom-equivalence and the
1364	  conjugate-transpose identity for the pullback-identity isomorphism shows the comparison
1365	  equals the \(\mathbf 1\)-component of that isomorphism.
1366	\end{proof}
1367	
1368	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1369	[Identity coherence at the free sheaf]
1370	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_id}
1371	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_id}
1372	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1373	  For the identity morphism \(\mathrm{id}_T\) and an index set \(I\), the free-pullback
1374	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*}(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1) \cong \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\) coincides
1375	  with the component at the free sheaf \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\) of the pullback-identity
1376	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*} \cong \mathrm{id}\).
1377	\end{lemma}
1378	
1379	\begin{proof}
1380	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1381	  Both sides are cocones on the coproduct \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\); by coproduct
1382	  extensionality it suffices to check equality after precomposing with each coprojection
1383	  \(\mathbf 1 \to \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\), which reduces to the one-element case
1384	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}.
1385	\end{proof}
1386	
1387	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1388	[Composite coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
1389	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1390	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1391	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp}
1392	  For composable scheme morphisms \(a : T'' \to T'\) and \(b : T' \to T\), the comparison
1393	  morphism of unit modules \((b \circ a)^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) factors as
1394	  \(a^{*}\) of the comparison for \(b\), the comparison for \(a\), and the component at
1395	  \(\mathbf{1}\) of the iterated-pullback comparison \(a^{*}b^{*} \cong (b\circ a)^{*}\)
1396	  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}).
1397	\end{lemma}
1398	
1399	\begin{proof}
1400	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp,
1401	    lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
1402	  This is the composite analogue of \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}: the mate
1403	  construction is compatible with composition of adjunctions, so the comparison for
1404	  \(b \circ a\) is the pasting of the comparisons for \(a\) and \(b\) reassociated by the
1405	  pseudofunctor comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. The compatibility of the mate
1406	  with the adjunction hom-equivalences is supplied by
1407	  \cref{lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}, the reusable conjugate-naturality engine. The
1408	  pentagon coherence of the iterated-pullback comparisons makes the factorisation
1409	  well-defined.
1410	\end{proof}
1411	
1412	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1413	[Mate/conjugate compatibility of the adjunction hom-equivalence]
1414	  \label{lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
1415	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
1416	  Let \(\mathrm{adj}_1, \mathrm{adj}_2\) be two adjunctions and \(\alpha\) a natural
1417	  transformation between their left adjoints. For a morphism \(f\) the hom-equivalence of
1418	  \(\mathrm{adj}_2\) applied to \(\alpha_c \circ f\) equals the hom-equivalence of
1419	  \(\mathrm{adj}_1\) applied to \(f\), post-composed with the component at \(d\) of the
1420	  conjugate (mate) of \(\alpha\). This is the general naturality of the conjugate
1421	  construction against the two hom-equivalences; it is the reusable engine behind
1422	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}.
1423	\end{lemma}
1424	
1425	\begin{proof}
1426	  Both sides unfold to the same pasting of unit/counit triangles for the two adjunctions;
1427	  the equality is the standard compatibility of the mate of \(\alpha\) with the
1428	  hom-set bijections.
1429	\end{proof}
1430	
1431	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1432	[Composite coherence at the free sheaf]
1433	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_comp}
1434	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_comp}
1435	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp, def:modules_pullbackComp}
1436	  The composite analogue of \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_id}: for composable \(a, b\) and an
1437	  index set \(I\), the free-pullback isomorphism for \(b \circ a\) at \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\)
1438	  factors through \(a^{*}\) of the free-pullback isomorphism for \(b\), the one for \(a\), and

1440	\end{lemma}
1441	
1442	\begin{proof}
1443	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1444	  By coproduct extensionality it suffices to check the identity after precomposing with each
1445	  coprojection \(\mathbf 1 \to \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\), which reduces to the one-element
1446	  composite coherence \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp} (with the base-ring sheaf

1449	
1450	Together \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id} and \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1451	upgrade to the corresponding free-sheaf coherences -- the free-pullback isomorphism at the
1452	identity equals the component of the pullback-identity isomorphism, and likewise for the
1453	composite -- by coproduct extensionality over the decomposition
1454	\(\mathcal{O}^{\,\oplus I} = \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\). These coherences discharge both
1455	functoriality laws (identity and composition) of the Grassmannian
1456	functor \cref{def:grassmannian_functor}.
1457	
1458	\begin{definition}
1459	
1460	\leanok
1461	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
1462	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
1463	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
1464	  \uses{def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:quot_functor, def:gr_rankQuotient,
1465	    lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1466	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1467	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
1468	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
1469	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1470	  % "represents the
1471	  %  contravariant functor
1472	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
1473	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
1474	  %  set of all equivalence classes
1475	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
1476	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
1477	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
1478	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
1479	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
1480	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
1481	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
1482	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
1483	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
1484	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
1485	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
1486	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
1487	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
1488	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
1489	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
1490	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
1491	  \[
1492	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
1493	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
1494	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
1495	  \]
1496	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
1497	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
1498	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
1499	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
1500	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
1501	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
1502	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
1503	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
1504	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
1505	\end{definition}
1506	
1507	\section{The universal property}
1508	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
1509	
1510	\begin{theorem}
1511	
1512	\leanok
1513	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
1514	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
1515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
1516	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
1517	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
1518	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
1519	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1520	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
1521	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
1522	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1523	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
1524	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
1525	  %  contravariant functor
1526	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
1527	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
1528	  %  set of all equivalence classes
1529	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
1530	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
1531	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
1532	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
1533	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
1534	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
1535	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
1536	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
1537	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
1538	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
1539	  \[
1540	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
1541	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
1542	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
1543	    \qquad
1544	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
1545	      \;\longmapsto\;
1546	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
1547	  \]
1548	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
1549	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
1550	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
1551	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
1552	  universal family.
1553	\end{theorem}
1554	
1555	\begin{proof}
1556	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
1557	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
1558	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
1559	    def:grassmannian_functor}
1560	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
1561	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
1562	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
1563	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
1564	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
1565	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
1566	
1567	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
1568	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
1569	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
1570	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
1571	  the composite
1572	  \[
1573	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
1574	      \overset{\ \iota_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
1575	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
1576	  \]
1577	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
1578	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
1579	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
1580	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
1581	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
1582	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
1583	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
1584	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
1585	
1586	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
1587	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
1588	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
1589	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
1590	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
1591	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
1592	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
1593	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
1594	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
1595	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
1596	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
1597	
1598	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
1599	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
1600	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
1601	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
1602	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
1603	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). Hence
1604	  \(\varphi_I\) and \(\varphi_J\) land in the overlap charts and agree there after
1605	  the transition \(\theta_{I,J}\), so by the cocycle condition
1606	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) the \(\varphi_I\) glue to a single morphism
1607	  \(\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). By
1608	  construction \(\varphi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F},
1609	  q \rangle\), since this holds on each \(T_I\) and the equivalence class is
1610	  determined by \(\ker(q)\).
1611	
1612	  \emph{Uniqueness.} If \(\psi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) also satisfies
1613	  \(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), then
1614	  on \(\psi^{-1}(U^I)\) the pulled-back tautological quotient \(\psi^{*}u^I\)
1615	  equals \(q\) in the \(I\)-trivialisation, which forces \(\psi^{*}X^I = M^I\) and
1616	  hence \(\psi = \varphi_I\) there; as the \(U^I\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\),
1617	  \(\psi = \varphi\). Thus \(\varphi\) is the unique morphism inducing
1618	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), and \(\varphi \mapsto \langle
1619	  \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u \rangle\) is bijective.
1620	
1621	  Therefore \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is a fine
1622	  moduli space: it represents \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\), so
1623	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
1624	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
1625	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChange.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChangeGlobal.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
5	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
6	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
7	
8	\section{Setup and motivation}
9	
10	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
11	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
12	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
13	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
14	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
15	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
16	out of scope here.
17	
18	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
19	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
20	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
21	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
22	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
23	\[
24	  \begin{array}{ccc}
25	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
26	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
27	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
28	  \end{array}
29	\]
30	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
31	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
32	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
33	
34	\section{The canonical base-change map}
35	
36	\begin{definition}\leanok
37	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
38	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
39	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
40	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
41	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
42	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
43	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
44	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
45	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
46	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
47	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
48	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
49	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
50	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
51	  change, I.}
52	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
53	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
54	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
55	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
56	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
57	  \[
58	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
59	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
60	  \]
61	  It is adjoint to the morphism
62	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
63	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
64	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
65	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
66	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
67	\end{definition}
68	
69	\section{The affine case}
70	
71	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for quasi-coherent sheaf morphisms}
72	
73	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
74	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
75	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
76	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
77	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
78	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
79	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
80	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
81	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
82	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
83	
84	\begin{lemma}\leanok
85	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
86	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
87	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
88	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
89	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
90	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
91	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
92	\end{lemma}
93	
94	\begin{proof}
95	
96	
97	
98	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
99	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
100	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the

1	\chapter{Regrouping isomorphism for the affine base-change tensor tower}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_RegroupHelper}
3	% This chapter blueprints the standalone, geometry-free helper that lives in
4	% AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/RegroupHelper.lean (1:1 slug). It is the pure
5	% tensor-algebra core of lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv in the FlatBaseChange
6	% chapter; it is split into its own compilation unit so the object-level
7	% ModuleCat iso can be obtained by a single LinearEquiv.toModuleIso application
8	% (the separate import normalises the `Module A (A ⊗[R] R')` instance diamond
9	% that blocks the same-file construction).
10	
11	\section{The R'-linear regrouping equivalence}
12	
13	\begin{lemma}
14	
15	\leanok
16	[Regrouping linear equivalence for the tensor tower]
17	  \label{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
18	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
19	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
20	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
21	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
22	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
23	  % L933--938).
24	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
25	  % equality
26	  % $$
27	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
28	  % $$
29	  % as $R'$-modules."
30	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
31	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
32	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\)
33	  an \(A\)-module. On the \emph{canonical} Mathlib instances (with \(R'\) acting on
34	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) through the \(\texttt{Algebra.TensorProduct.rightAlgebra}\)
35	  factor, i.e.\ \(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)) there is a bundled
36	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
37	  \[
38	    \operatorname{regroup} :
39	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
40	  \]
41	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the affine base-change source. On generators
42	  it sends \((a \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes (a \cdot m)\); in
43	  particular \((1 \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\), with inverse
44	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
45	  hypothesis.
46	\end{lemma}
47	
48	\begin{proof}

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	
6	\section{Setup and motivation}
7	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
8	
9	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
10	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
11	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
12	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
13	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
14	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
15	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
16	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
17	single non-zero element of the base ring.
18	
19	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
20	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
21	\(\F\).
22	
23	\section{Generic flatness}
24	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
25	
26	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
27	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
28	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
29	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
30	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
31	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
32	non-zero element of the base ring.
33	
34	\begin{theorem}
35	
36	
37	
38	\leanok
39	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
40	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
41	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
42	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
43	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
44	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
45	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
48	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
49	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
50	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."

7	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
8	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
9	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
10	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
12	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
13	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
14	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
15	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
16	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/GradedHilbertSerre.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;

48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).

34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).

52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and

1510-  Let \(Y\) be an open of a scheme and \(\F\) a sheaf of modules on \(Y\). Then
1511-  \(\F\) is globally generated by a finite family of sections if and only if there is a
1512-  finite index set \(I\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_Y\)-modules
1513-  \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F\). No quasi-coherence of \(\F\) is assumed:
1514-  the statement holds for arbitrary sheaves of modules on \(Y\), and applies in particular
1515-  to the restrictions \(\F|_{D(g)}\) arising in the cover assembly.
1516-\end{lemma}
1517-
1518-\begin{proof}
1519-  A finite family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) of \(\F|_Y\) is the same datum as an
1520-  \(\OO_Y\)-linear map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) sending the \(i\)-th basis section to
1521-  \(s_i\). The family generates \(\F|_Y\) (the induced map is surjective on every stalk)
1522-  exactly when this map is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules. Thus a finite generating
1523-  family yields a finite-free epimorphism, and conversely the images of the basis sections
1524-  of any epimorphism \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_Y\) with \(I\) finite form a
1525-  finite generating family.
1526-\end{proof}
